metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC covering the apply_inbound_records.py wiring: e.g., 'Given an inbound Jira ticket with an assignee accountId X and displayName Y, running the inbound apply creates a placeholder identity for (jira, X) and stores its id on the ticket attribution, and a second apply of the same ticket reuses the same identity id (count == 1)'; also add an AC for the missing-accountId fallback (apply completes without error, falls back to name-only attribution).
findings.finding: Section 3 (apply_inbound_records.py wiring) is listed in scope and file_impact but has zero acceptance criteria covering it — the inbound Jira user resolution wiring and its best-effort fallback are unverified by any AC.
findings.location: Section 3 ('Inbound reconciler wiring') and Acceptance Criteria section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `reporter` is in scope for `ensure_identity_for` wiring: if yes, add it explicitly to `file_impact` and What §3 since it does not currently exist in `apply_inbound_records.py`; if no, remove 'reporter' from the wiring description to avoid misleading the implementer.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'assignee/reporter' are both currently resolved via `_extract_name` in `apply_inbound_records.py`, but a file read and search of that file find NO `reporter` field handling anywhere in `apply_inbound_records.py`. Only `assignee` is handled via `_extract_name`. The plan's wiring section says to resolve 'the inbound user' for 'assignee/reporter' but reporter is not a field in the current inbound-create or inbound-update paths.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify Section 3: either remove `reporter` from the wiring description (limiting scope to `assignee` only, which is what actually exists at lines 113/258), or enumerate a new reporter extraction site in `file_impact` and add an AC covering it.
findings.finding: The plan says 'assignee/reporter — currently `_extract_name(fields["assignee"])` at ~lines 113/258' implying both roles are already extracted there, but `reporter` does not appear anywhere in `apply_inbound_records.py`. Lines 113 and 258 cover only the `assignee` field. There is no existing reporter extraction site to wire `ensure_identity_for` into, so the plan's Section 3 description either (a) overstates the existing scope of those lines, or (b) silently requires adding a net-new reporter extraction site that is not enumerated in `file_impact`.
findings.suggested_fix: In Section 3, change 'assignee/reporter' to 'assignee' to match the actual extraction sites at ~lines 113/258; add a separate bullet if reporter minting is in scope, naming the new site where reporter would be extracted from `fields.get("reporter")` and listing it in `file_impact`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend `create_identity_core` (and its `create_identity` wrapper in `_lib_writes.py`) to accept a `tags` list so that `ensure_identity_for` can set the `placeholder` tag at mint time; alternatively, specify that a post-create `tag()` call is used and document it explicitly in the What section.
findings.finding: The plan's placeholder-tag mechanism cannot be implemented as written: create_identity_core (identity.py:157–180) calls create_core() without a `tags` argument, and create_identity in _lib_writes.py also has no `tags` parameter. Since is_placeholder() is specified to check for the 'placeholder' tag in compiled state — and the reducer surfaces tags from the CREATE event (reducer/_processors.py:194) — the tag cannot be set at mint time without either extending create_identity_core to accept and forward a `tags` list, or issuing a separate post-create tag() call. Neither path is specified, leaving the is_placeholder() helper without a reliable foundation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `tags: list[str] | None = None` to `create_identity_core`'s signature and forward it to `create_core(... tags=tags ...)`, then propagate the same parameter through `create_identity` in `_lib_writes.py`. In `ensure_identity_for`, call `create_identity(..., tags=['placeholder'])` directly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one acceptance criterion covering Section 3's reconciler wiring: given an inbound Jira record with a known accountId, ensure_identity_for is invoked and a placeholder identity is created; and given a record with no accountId, the inbound apply completes without error (best-effort fallback).
findings.finding: Section 3 (inbound reconciler wiring of apply_inbound_records.py) is a described deliverable with no covering acceptance criterion — no AC tests or verifies that ensure_identity_for is actually called for Jira assignee/reporter fields, that the best-effort fallback on missing accountId works, or that the wiring produces any observable outcome.
findings.location: Section 3 'Inbound reconciler wiring' and Acceptance Criteria block
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC4: 'When apply_inbound_records processes a Jira ticket whose assignee has an accountId, a placeholder identity is created/reused (ensure_identity_for called with provider="jira", external_id=<accountId>). When the accountId field is absent, the apply completes without raising and falls back to name-only attribution (test both branches).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs (and tests) for the two stated failure/fallback contracts: (a) when `resolve_mapping` raises/fails, `ensure_identity_for` falls through to mint without raising; (b) when the inbound Jira user field has no `accountId`, `apply_inbound_records` falls back to name-only behavior without raising or failing the inbound apply.
findings.finding: The plan states 'Never raises for a lookup problem (a resolve failure falls through to mint)' and 'a missing accountId falls back to the name-only behavior without failing the inbound apply,' but neither behavioral contract has a corresponding AC or test. The ACs cover only happy paths (successful mint, idempotent re-run, two-external-id independence) and omit all failure/fallback paths.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section; Section 1 ('Never raises for a lookup problem'); Section 3 ('a missing accountId falls back to the name-only behavior without failing the inbound apply')
findings.suggested_fix:
Add two ACs:
1. 'If `resolve_mapping` raises or returns no result, `ensure_identity_for` mints a placeholder and does NOT propagate the exception — a test injects a resolver that raises and asserts the returned id is a valid placeholder id.'
2. 'If the inbound Jira user field lacks an `accountId`, `apply_inbound_records` completes without error and falls back to `displayName`-only attribution — a test feeds a user dict with no `accountId` through the inbound apply and asserts no exception and the displayName is preserved.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `create_identity` (or `create_identity_core`) will be extended to accept a `tags` parameter so `ensure_identity_for` can attach the `placeholder` tag at mint time; document this extension in `file_impact` and the 'What' section.
findings.finding: The plan calls `create_identity(name=..., email="", mappings=[...], ...)` to mint a placeholder, and separately says the placeholder is 'marked as a placeholder via a `placeholder` tag'. But the lib-layer `create_identity` function (`_lib_writes.py:168`) does not expose a `tags` parameter — its signature is `(name, email, mappings, keys, *, repo_root, return_alias)`. The underlying `create_identity_core` → `create_core` does accept `tags`, but that parameter is not plumbed through the public API the plan names.
findings.location: Section 'What / 1. Resolve-or-mint seam' — `create_identity(name=display_name or external_id, email="", mappings=[...])` + `file_impact: src/rebar/__init__.py + src/rebar/_lib_writes.py`
findings.suggested_fix: In `file_impact` (and the 'What' section), explicitly note that `_lib_writes.create_identity` / `create_identity_core` must be extended with a `tags: list[str] | None = None` parameter that is forwarded to `create_core`. This is a one-line change to each wrapper and is low-risk since `create_core` already supports `tags`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that ticket 264f (`resolve_mapping`) is fully committed to the branch before this plan's implementation begins, or add `resolve_mapping` to this plan's `file_impact` as a prerequisite that must be implemented inline.
findings.finding: `resolve_mapping` — which `ensure_identity_for` calls as its resolve-first step — does not exist in `src/rebar/_commands/identity.py` at HEAD. The plan asserts it is 'committed on this branch' (via stacked dep 264f), but ticket 264f has status `in_progress`, not closed, and a grep of `identity.py` finds zero occurrences of `resolve_mapping`. If 264f is not yet committed, the core idempotency mechanism of `ensure_identity_for` (resolve-first, then conditionally mint) has no foundation in the current codebase.
findings.location: Section 1 ('Resolve-or-mint seam') / Dependencies block ('committed on this branch')
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) confirm 264f is merged/committed and cite its HEAD SHA in the Dependencies block, or (b) add `resolve_mapping` to this plan's What §1 and `file_impact` so it is implemented here rather than assumed from a still-open ticket.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's description of `_extract_name` to note it returns `val.get('name') or val.get('displayName')` (name-first), and confirm that for Jira Cloud user objects the `displayName` field is the one that will be passed as `display_name` to `ensure_identity_for`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `_extract_name` 'pulls only the `displayName`' from Jira user fields. In reality, `_extract_name` in `inbound_translate.py` (line 263–272) returns `val.get('name') or val.get('displayName') or default` — it tries `.name` first, then `.displayName`. For Jira assignee objects (`{accountId, displayName, emailAddress}`), `.name` is absent so `.displayName` is used, but for other Jira objects (e.g. issuetype, status), `.name` is used. This means the plan's description of the function's behavior is inaccurate, which could cause confusion about what `displayName` value `ensure_identity_for` actually receives.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'which pulls only the `displayName`' to 'which returns `.name` or `.displayName` (in that priority) from the Jira user object — for Jira Cloud assignees this is the `displayName`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in §1 whether `ensure_identity_for` removes the `placeholder` tag during title-upgrade (automatic graduation) or always leaves tag removal to explicit operator action, and document the decision rule alongside the `is_placeholder` helper.
findings.finding: The upgrade decision rule — 'Optionally UPGRADE: if that identity is still a placeholder and `display_name` is non-empty and differs from its current title, update the title in place (`edit_ticket`)' — is stated in §1 but the conditions for tag removal (graduating the identity from placeholder to real) are only described in §2 as an operator action ('removing the `placeholder` tag'). No code path in `ensure_identity_for` removes the placeholder tag, yet AC #2 implies re-running with an enriched display_name fully upgrades the record. The decision rule for when the placeholder tag should be removed by `ensure_identity_for` vs. left for operator action is never specified.
Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in §1 whether placeholder tagging is atomic (extend `create_identity_core` to accept `tags=` and pass it through to `create_core`) or a separate TAG_DELTA step, and document the chosen approach.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify HOW the `placeholder` tag is attached at mint time. `create_identity_core` in the existing codebase does not pass a `tags` argument to `create_core` (confirmed by code reading: the call omits `tags=`). The plan says 'marked as a placeholder via a `placeholder` tag' at creation but leaves the executor to decide between (a) extending `create_identity_core` to accept and forward a `tags` parameter, or (b) issuing a separate TAG_DELTA event immediately after the CREATE. This is a two-step mutation with no specified approach.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `create_identity_core` to accept an optional `tags: list[str] | None = None` argument and forward it to `create_core(... tags=tags ...)`, then call it with `tags=[PLACEHOLDER_TAG]` from `ensure_identity_for`. This keeps the mint atomic (single CREATE event).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove 'Optionally' from the upgrade description in Section 1 or replace it with 'and UPGRADE' to match AC #2's mandatory upgrade wording.
findings.finding: 'Optionally UPGRADE' in Section 1 signals the title-upgrade is discretionary, but AC #2 mandates it ('which upgrades the title in place'), creating a conflicting signal an executor may misread as a genuine opt-in.
findings.location: Section 1 ('Optionally UPGRADE: if that identity is still a placeholder…') vs. AC #2
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'Optionally UPGRADE: if that identity is still a placeholder and display_name is non-empty and differs from its current title, update the title in place (edit_ticket) — the upgrade path.' to 'UPGRADE if the resolved identity is still a placeholder and display_name is non-empty and differs from its current title: update the title in place via edit_ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the placeholder-identity discipline: keyed on stable external_id (never email), provider-neutral, `placeholder` tag as the ghost discriminator, and upgrade-in-place semantics — so future providers and UX consumers have a written contract to follow.
findings.finding: The novel architectural decision — placeholder/ghost identity discipline keyed on stable external_id, with in-place upgrade and a `placeholder` tag as the real/ghost discriminator — is not captured in any ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update. This pattern establishes a durable contract that downstream consumers (validation, UX, future providers) must respect.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (or a `## Identity placeholder discipline` section to CLAUDE.md) covering: (1) the external_id-keyed mint rule, (2) the `placeholder` tag as the sole ghost discriminator, (3) the upgrade-in-place contract, and (4) the additive/best-effort wiring expectation. Reference it from `identity.py`'s module docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability to `ensure_identity_for`: emit a structured log line (at DEBUG/INFO) on placeholder mint, on in-place upgrade, and on best-effort fallback (missing accountId), so operators can monitor placeholder accumulation and upgrade progress.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new stateful transitions (mint-placeholder, upgrade-placeholder-in-place) with no observability plan — no log lines, metrics, or traces are specified for these paths. Operators have no way to detect a stuck upgrade, a runaway mint loop, or the current count of placeholder identities.
findings.location: ## What section 1 / ## What section 3 / Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: In `ensure_identity_for`, add `logger.info('minted placeholder identity', provider=provider, external_id=external_id, identity_id=new_id)` and `logger.info('upgraded placeholder identity', ...)`. In the inbound wiring fallback, `logger.debug('no accountId for Jira user, falling back to name-only', field=...)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the placeholder retirement path: specify either a CLI command, a reconciler step, or documented operator procedure for removing the `placeholder` tag (graduating to a real identity), and add at least one AC or scope note covering this lifecycle exit.
findings.finding: The placeholder retirement path (removing the `placeholder` tag to 'graduate' an identity from ghost to real) is described only as 'an operator enriching the identity (email/keys) and removing the placeholder tag' — no tooling, command, or automated trigger is named. Without a defined retirement mechanism, placeholder identities can accumulate indefinitely with no clear path to cleanup.
findings.location: Section 2 (Placeholder → real upgrade) and Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a scope note or AC: 'A placeholder is retired (tag removed) when an operator runs `rebar identity upgrade <id> --email <email>` or equivalent, which removes the `placeholder` tag and sets the email field. The retirement is idempotent.' Reference this in the file_impact for identity.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a mechanism to enforce the single-writer guarantee (e.g., unique constraint on (provider, external_id) in the identity store, or an explicit advisory note that the reconciler MUST remain single-writer with a pointer to where that is enforced), or document the failure mode and recovery procedure if a duplicate is minted.
findings.finding: The plan asserts concurrency safety by scoping to 'single-writer, sequential' execution, but specifies no enforcement mechanism (lock, queue, unique DB constraint, or process-level guarantee) that prevents two concurrent calls to `ensure_identity_for` with the same (provider, external_id) from both passing the resolve check and both minting a placeholder — a classic check-then-act TOCTOU race.
findings.location: Scope section ('Concurrency: minting is idempotent for SEQUENTIAL re-runs (resolve-first); the reconciler applies inbound records single-writer, so no concurrent double-mint of the same accountId is expected.')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'Optionally UPGRADE' in Section 1 with a deterministic condition: 'UPGRADE (when the resolved identity is still a placeholder and display_name is non-empty and differs from its current title, update the title via edit_ticket)' — making the upgrade mandatory when conditions are met, consistent with AC2.
findings.finding: Section 1 says 'Optionally UPGRADE: if that identity is still a placeholder and display_name is non-empty and differs…' (the word 'Optionally' implies the upgrade may be skipped), but AC2 requires 'a second call supplies a different display_name (which upgrades the title in place)' — implying the upgrade is mandatory when conditions are met. This ambiguity could cause an implementer to skip the upgrade and still pass Section 1 while failing AC2.
findings.location: Section 1 ('Optionally UPGRADE') vs. AC2 ('upgrades the title in place')
findings.suggested_fix: Change Section 1 wording from 'Optionally UPGRADE: if that identity is still a placeholder and display_name is non-empty and differs from its current title, update the title in place (edit_ticket) — the upgrade path.' to 'UPGRADE when the resolved identity is still a placeholder and display_name is non-empty and differs from its current title: update the title in place via edit_ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and document the post-partial-failure schema state for the two-step placeholder upgrade (title edit + tag removal): assert whether each step is individually idempotent, specify the safe intermediate state, and add a compensating-forward note (or DATA LOSS RISK annotation) so that a re-run of ensure_identity_for always converges to a consistent record regardless of where a prior run crashed.
findings.finding: The in-place placeholder-upgrade path (`edit_ticket` to update title + eventual `placeholder` tag removal) is a two-step mutation with no described atomicity, rollback path, or post-partial-failure schema state assertion. If the process crashes or errors between the two steps, the identity record is left in an ambiguous intermediate state (e.g. title updated but still tagged placeholder, or tag stripped but title stale). The plan does not specify what the record looks like after a partial failure, nor does it describe a compensating-forward strategy or back-out procedure.
findings.location: What § 1 (ensure_identity_for upgrade path) and § 2 (Placeholder → real upgrade)
findings.suggested_fix: Make the upgrade idempotent at each step independently: (1) always re-apply the title update if title differs (already idempotent via edit_ticket); (2) only remove the placeholder tag as a final step after confirming the record has a non-empty email or other real-identity signal — and document that a record with an updated title but still-present placeholder tag is a valid, safe intermediate state that a re-run will complete. Add a note in the plan: 'A partially-upgraded record (title updated, tag still present) is safe — re-running ensure_identity_for will complete the tag removal; no DATA LOSS RISK on partial failure.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and canonicalize the placeholder tag string literal (e.g., a module-level constant `PLACEHOLDER_TAG = "placeholder"`) in §1 so both the mint path and `is_placeholder` predicate reference the same value.
findings.finding: The `placeholder` tag string value — the discriminator for `is_placeholder()` and the tag removed during upgrade — is never canonicalized in the plan. The plan says 'marked as a placeholder via a `placeholder` tag' and '`is_placeholder` (the `placeholder` tag is present)' using informal prose, but never defines the canonical tag-name constant (e.g., `"placeholder"`, `"ghost"`, or something else). An executor must invent the exact string, creating a risk of mismatched checks between the mint path and the `is_placeholder` predicate.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to §1: 'The placeholder tag string is the module-level constant `PLACEHOLDER_TAG = "placeholder"` in `identity.py`; both the mint (`add_tags=[PLACEHOLDER_TAG]`) and `is_placeholder` check (`PLACEHOLDER_TAG in state.get("tags", [])`) MUST reference this constant.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
